narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a white male applicant with a middle-income family background, your law school application was carefully evaluated using a predictive model that assesses the likelihood of first-time success on the bar exam based on academic and personal profiles like yours. The model estimates your probability of failing the bar exam at 41.4%, which is high enough to trigger an automatic rejection, as the school prioritizes admitting students predicted to pass on their first attempt. This decision was driven primarily by your Year 1 school decile ranking of 4th, which stood out as the top factor pushing the prediction toward failure—it's below the typical student's 6th decile, signaling weaker early performance compared to most applicants. On a more positive note, your Year 3 decile of 4th (also below the average 5th or 6th) actually worked in your favor more than it hurt, helping pull the prediction toward success despite the lower rank. Your middle family income level emerged as the strongest positive influence overall, as it aligns with brackets that correlate with bar passage in the dataset, giving you an edge over lower-income peers. Being a full-time student further supported a passing prediction, matching the norm for 93% of students who succeed. Your LSAT score of 30 and undergraduate GPA of 2.90 played smaller roles but leaned positive, as they weren't drastically below averages of 37 and 3.2, respectively, keeping the odds from worsening. Compared to typical students, your profile shows solid later-year stability and supportive circumstances, but that early decile dip was the key mismatch that tipped the scales. While disappointing, this isn't a judgment on your potential—it's just one model's data-driven call. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost it toward 37 or higher, strengthening your early academic record through additional coursework or explanations in a reapplications, and highlighting your full-time commitment and family stability in a personal statement. Many students in similar situations improve these metrics and gain admission elsewhere; you're already ahead in key areas, so targeted steps could change the outcome next time. Keep pushing—you've got this.
